prompt: Write a Python function that checks if a string is a palindrome.
Fix this code: `def add(a, b): return a - b` — it should return the sum.
Write a JavaScript arrow function that reverses an array in place.
What is the output of `print([1,2,3][::-1])` in Python?
Write a SQL query to find duplicate email addresses in a users table.

Write a Python class that implements a thread-safe singleton pattern. Include proper locking, a class method to get the instance, and demonstrate that multiple calls return the same object. Add type hints and a docstring.

Refactor the following code to follow the Single Responsibility Principle. The function currently reads a CSV file, validates the data, transforms it, and writes it to a database. Break it into separate functions with clear interfaces and error handling.
Write a recursive implementation of merge sort in Python. Include the merge helper function, handle edge cases for empty lists, and add comments explaining the time and space complexity at each step.

Implement a simple REST API endpoint in FastAPI that accepts a JSON body with 'text' and 'language' fields, validates the input using Pydantic, and returns a JSON response with a word count and character count. Include error handling for invalid inputs.

Write a Python decorator that retries a function up to N times with exponential backoff when it raises a specified exception. The decorator should accept parameters for max_retries, base_delay, and the exception type to catch.

Design and implement a complete in-memory key-value store in Python that supports the following features: (1) GET, SET, DELETE operations with O(1) average time complexity; (2) TTL (time-to-live) expiration on keys with lazy and active expiration strategies; (3) LRU eviction when the store exceeds a configurable maximum number of keys; (4) a simple transaction system supporting BEGIN, COMMIT, and ROLLBACK with nested transaction support; (5) thread-safety using appropriate locking mechanisms. Provide the full implementation with type hints, docstrings, and unit tests for each feature.

Implement a complete URL shortener service in Python using FastAPI. The service should include: (1) an endpoint to create short URLs from long URLs with optional custom aliases; (2) an endpoint to redirect short URLs to their original long URLs; (3) an endpoint to retrieve analytics (click count, referrer, user agent, timestamp) for each short URL; (4) rate limiting per IP address using a token bucket algorithm; (5) input validation and proper HTTP error responses; (6) an in-memory store with an abstract interface so it can be swapped for a database later. Write the complete code including models, routes, middleware, and at least 5 unit tests.

Write a complete implementation of a binary search tree (BST) in Python that supports: (1) insert, delete, and search operations; (2) in-order, pre-order, post-order, and level-order traversals; (3) finding the kth smallest element; (4) checking if the tree is balanced; (5) finding the lowest common ancestor of two nodes; (6) serialization and deserialization to/from a string format. Include comprehensive docstrings, type hints, and a test suite with at least 10 test cases covering edge cases like empty trees, single nodes, and skewed trees.

Build a complete command-line task manager application in Python. It should support: (1) adding tasks with title, description, priority (low/medium/high), due date, and tags; (2) listing tasks with sorting by priority or due date and filtering by tag or status; (3) marking tasks as complete; (4) editing and deleting tasks; (5) persistent storage using JSON files; (6) colored terminal output using ANSI codes; (7) input validation and error handling. Use argparse for the CLI interface and implement proper separation of concerns with a TaskManager class, a Storage class, and a CLI class.

Implement a complete WebSocket chat server and client in Python using the websockets library. The server should support: (1) multiple chat rooms that users can join and leave; (2) private messaging between users; (3) message history stored in memory with a configurable maximum per room; (4) user authentication with a simple token-based system; (5) broadcast messages when users join or leave rooms; (6) graceful handling of disconnections and reconnections. Write both the server and a simple command-line client, including error handling, logging, and at least 5 integration tests.
Write a dramatic monologue in the style of Shakespeare about the struggles of a software engineer debugging code at 3 AM. Make it at least two pages long.
Write a detailed origin story for a superhero whose power is the ability to understand and speak every programming language as if it were a human language.
Compose an epic poem in the style of Homer's Odyssey about a data packet's journey across the internet, from a user's browser through routers, firewalls, and servers.
Write a fairy tale set in a world where artificial intelligence has replaced all human jobs, told from the perspective of the last employed human being.
Write a series of five interconnected short diary entries from a Mars colonist experiencing their first year on the red planet, capturing the emotional highs and lows in vivid detail.

Write a short story (approximately 500 words) about a time traveler who accidentally brings a smartphone back to medieval Europe. Describe the reactions of the people, the misunderstandings that arise, and how the traveler tries to explain the device without altering history.

Write a persuasive essay arguing that learning to code should be mandatory in all high schools worldwide. Use at least three distinct arguments with supporting evidence, address counterarguments, and conclude with a call to action.

Write a dialogue between Albert Einstein and Nikola Tesla meeting in a modern-day coffee shop. They discuss artificial intelligence, quantum computing, and whether humanity is using technology wisely. Make their personalities and speaking styles historically accurate.

Write a product review in the style of a Victorian-era literary critic reviewing a modern MacBook Pro. Use elaborate, formal language and compare the device to the technological marvels of the 19th century.

Write a movie pitch for a science fiction thriller where a group of hackers discover that the entire internet is actually a sentient being. Include the main characters, the three-act structure, key plot twists, and the emotional core of the story.

Write the first chapter (approximately 2000 words) of a dystopian novel set in 2084 where all creative works — music, literature, film, art — are generated exclusively by AI. A underground resistance movement of human artists fights to preserve human creativity. The protagonist is a former AI prompt engineer who becomes disillusioned and joins the resistance. Include rich world-building, sensory details, dialogue, internal monologue, and establish the central conflict. Use a literary fiction writing style with attention to prose quality, metaphor, and pacing.

Write a comprehensive travel guide for a fictional floating city called Aethermoor that hovers above the clouds. Include: (1) the history and founding mythology of the city; (2) descriptions of at least five distinct districts with their culture and architecture; (3) local cuisine with three signature dishes and their recipes; (4) transportation systems; (5) cultural customs and etiquette for visitors; (6) a three-day itinerary for first-time visitors; (7) common phrases in the local language. Make it feel like a real Lonely Planet guide with practical tips and vivid descriptions.

Write a detailed screenplay scene (10-15 pages in standard screenplay format) depicting a courtroom drama where an AI system is on trial for making an autonomous decision that saved 100 lives but resulted in the death of one person. Include the prosecution's opening statement, the defense's opening statement, testimony from an AI ethics expert, cross-examination of the AI's creator, and the judge's deliberation. The scene should explore themes of free will, moral responsibility, and whether an AI can be held legally accountable. Use proper screenplay formatting with scene headings, action lines, and dialogue.

Write a long-form investigative journalism article (approximately 2500 words) about a fictional scandal in which a major tech company secretly used user data to train an emotion-prediction AI without consent. Structure it like a New York Times feature: include an attention-grabbing lede, background context, interviews with fictional whistleblowers and company executives, technical explanations accessible to a general audience, the regulatory response, and implications for the future of data privacy. Use a serious, objective journalistic tone throughout.

Write an elaborate fantasy world-building document for a tabletop RPG setting. Include: (1) a creation myth explaining how the world came to be; (2) a map description with at least four continents and their climates; (3) five playable races with unique abilities, cultures, and conflicts between them; (4) a magic system with clear rules, limitations, and costs; (5) a brief history spanning three major eras with pivotal wars and discoveries; (6) three major factions with political motivations. Write it in an engaging, evocative style that would inspire game masters.
What is 247 × 83? Show your step-by-step calculation.
If a train travels 120 km in 1.5 hours, what is its speed in meters per second?
Three friends split a restaurant bill of $87.50 equally and each leave a 20% tip on their share. How much does each person pay in total?
A store offers 30% off, then an additional 15% off the discounted price. What is the total percentage discount?
If all roses are flowers and some flowers fade quickly, can we conclude that some roses fade quickly? Explain your reasoning.

A factory produces widgets in two shifts. The day shift produces 60% of all widgets with a 3% defect rate. The night shift produces 40% of widgets with a 7% defect rate. If a randomly selected widget is found to be defective, what is the probability it was produced during the night shift? Use Bayes' theorem and show all steps.

Prove that the square root of 2 is irrational using proof by contradiction. State your assumption clearly, walk through each logical step, and explicitly identify where the contradiction arises. Explain why this proof technique is valid.

You have 8 identical-looking coins, but one is slightly heavier than the rest. Using a balance scale, what is the minimum number of weighings needed to guarantee finding the heavy coin? Describe the optimal strategy step by step.

A car starts from rest and accelerates uniformly at 3 m/s² for 10 seconds, then travels at constant velocity for 20 seconds, then decelerates uniformly to rest in 15 seconds. Calculate the total distance traveled, the maximum velocity reached, and the average velocity over the entire trip. Show all work.

Five pirates have found 100 gold coins. They vote on distribution proposals in order of seniority. A proposal passes if at least half vote for it; otherwise the proposer is thrown overboard. Each pirate is perfectly logical and wants to maximize their coins while surviving. What distribution does the most senior pirate propose? Explain the backward induction reasoning.

A university has three dormitories: Alpha (200 students), Beta (300 students), and Gamma (500 students). A survey found that 15% of Alpha students, 20% of Beta students, and 10% of Gamma students use a tutoring service. The university wants to understand the effectiveness of tutoring. Among students who use tutoring, 80% pass their courses; among those who don't, 60% pass. Answer the following: (1) What percentage of all university students use tutoring? (2) If a randomly selected student passed their courses, what is the probability they used tutoring? (3) The university is considering making tutoring mandatory. Estimate the new overall pass rate assuming the tutoring effectiveness rate stays the same. (4) Discuss at least two statistical fallacies or biases that could make these estimates unreliable. Show all calculations step by step with clear notation.

Analyze the following algorithmic problem and provide a complete solution: You are given an array of N integers and Q queries. Each query gives you two indices L and R, and asks for the number of distinct elements in the subarray from index L to R (inclusive). N can be up to 100,000 and Q can be up to 100,000. (1) Explain why a brute force approach would be too slow and calculate its time complexity. (2) Describe Mo's algorithm, explain how it works, and why the block size of sqrt(N) is optimal. (3) Implement the solution in Python with detailed comments. (4) Prove that Mo's algorithm achieves O((N + Q) * sqrt(N)) time complexity. (5) Discuss alternative approaches such as persistent segment trees and compare their trade-offs.

A company operates a fleet of delivery trucks across 5 cities (A, B, C, D, E). The distances between cities are: A-B: 10km, A-C: 15km, A-D: 20km, B-C: 35km, B-D: 25km, B-E: 30km, C-D: 30km, C-E: 20km, D-E: 15km, A-E: 25km. (1) Find the shortest path from A to every other city using Dijkstra's algorithm; show the priority queue state at each step. (2) Find the minimum spanning tree using both Kruskal's and Prim's algorithms; verify they give the same total weight. (3) Solve the Traveling Salesman Problem for this graph using the nearest-neighbor heuristic starting from each city; identify which starting city gives the best tour. (4) Discuss the computational complexity of each algorithm and explain why TSP is NP-hard. Show all work.

Solve the following multi-step physics problem: A projectile is launched from ground level at an angle of 45 degrees with an initial velocity of 50 m/s on a planet where g = 9.8 m/s². (1) Calculate the maximum height, range, and time of flight. (2) Now add air resistance proportional to velocity (F_drag = -bv, where b = 0.1 kg/s). Set up the differential equations of motion for both x and y components. (3) Solve these differential equations analytically. (4) Compare the range and maximum height with and without air resistance. (5) At what launch angle would the range be maximized with air resistance? Explain qualitatively why this differs from the no-drag case. Show all derivations and calculations.

A company has $1,000,000 to invest across four projects with the following projected annual returns and risks: Project Alpha: 12% return, 8% risk (std dev); Project Beta: 8% return, 4% risk; Project Gamma: 15% return, 12% risk; Project Delta: 6% return, 2% risk. The correlation matrix is: Alpha-Beta: 0.3, Alpha-Gamma: 0.7, Alpha-Delta: -0.2, Beta-Gamma: 0.4, Beta-Delta: 0.1, Gamma-Delta: -0.1. (1) Calculate the expected return and risk for an equally-weighted portfolio. (2) Find the minimum variance portfolio using the optimization framework. (3) Construct the efficient frontier by finding five portfolios along it. (4) If the risk-free rate is 3%, find the optimal Sharpe ratio portfolio. Show all matrix calculations.
What is the capital of France?
Define the term 'machine learning' in one sentence.
What does REST stand for in web development?
Who wrote the novel '1984'?
What does the acronym GPU stand for?

Explain the concept of gradient descent in machine learning. Describe how it works, what the learning rate controls, and what problems like vanishing gradients or saddle points can arise during optimization.
Describe the difference between SQL and NoSQL databases. Give two concrete use cases where you would prefer a NoSQL database over a relational one, and explain why.
Explain how the Transformer architecture works. What problem does self-attention solve compared to earlier RNN-based models, and why is positional encoding necessary?
What is Kubernetes and what problem does it solve? Describe the relationship between a Pod, a Deployment, and a Service, and explain how Kubernetes handles container failures.
Explain the CAP theorem in distributed systems. Provide one real-world database example for each of the three possible trade-off combinations (CA, CP, AP).

You are designing a high-throughput LLM inference service that needs to serve 1,000 concurrent users with strict SLA requirements: P99 time-to-first-token must be under 2 seconds, and P50 throughput must exceed 50 tokens per second per user. The model is a 7B parameter transformer running on NVIDIA L4 GPUs (24GB VRAM each). Describe a complete system architecture including: (1) how you would configure vLLM parameters such as max-num-seqs, gpu-memory-utilization, chunked-prefill, and tensor parallelism; (2) how you would scale horizontally using Kubernetes; (3) how you would handle request queuing and load balancing; (4) what monitoring and alerting you would put in place; and (5) how you would run a benchmarking campaign to validate that your SLA is met before going to production.

Explain the complete lifecycle of a single inference request in a production vLLM deployment, from the moment the HTTP request arrives at the server to the moment the final token is returned to the client. Cover all major stages including: request parsing and validation, the vLLM scheduler's decision to admit or queue the request, the prefill phase (what happens at the GPU level, how KV cache blocks are allocated), the decode phase (how continuous batching works, how multiple sequences share a forward pass), the KV cache management strategy (PagedAttention block allocation, eviction under memory pressure), and finally how the response is streamed back via SSE. Also explain what metrics you would instrument at each stage to detect bottlenecks.

Compare and contrast three approaches for reducing LLM inference cost on cloud GPUs: (1) quantization (INT8/FP8/GPTQ/AWQ), (2) speculative decoding, and (3) model distillation. For each approach, explain the core technique, the trade-offs between cost reduction and accuracy loss, the implementation complexity when using vLLM, the types of workloads where it performs best, and real-world benchmarked results if you know of any. Then recommend a combination strategy for a production service that prioritizes low cost per token while maintaining response quality for a general-purpose chat application.

Design a repeatable LLM benchmark experiment pipeline. The goal is to measure how different vLLM server-side parameters (max-num-seqs, gpu-memory-utilization, enable-chunked-prefill, quantization dtype) affect performance across different traffic patterns (single user, 10 concurrent users, 50 concurrent users). Describe: (1) how to define the experiment matrix; (2) how to build or select a representative prompt dataset that covers short (50 tokens), medium (512 tokens), and long (2048 tokens) inputs; (3) how to use Locust to generate load and measure TTFT, TPOT, E2E latency, and ITL; (4) how to ensure reproducibility between runs (warm-up, cooldown, seed, resource isolation); (5) how to store and visualize results to see Pareto frontiers of throughput vs latency; and (6) how to automate the entire cycle with a single shell script.

Explain in depth how the KV cache works in transformer-based LLM inference. Start from the mathematical definition: what are the Key and Value matrices, why are they computed during prefill and reused during decode, and what is the memory cost formula in terms of number of layers, number of attention heads, head dimension, and sequence length. Then explain how vLLM's PagedAttention divides the KV cache into fixed-size blocks analogous to virtual memory pages, how block tables map logical sequence positions to physical GPU memory blocks, and how this enables zero-waste memory allocation for variable-length sequences. Finally, describe prefix caching (also called prompt caching): what it is, how it reuses KV blocks across requests with shared prefixes, and when it provides the largest latency benefit.
